extra.reasoning:
The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) is violated by transition [t10], which sets sent := true when (opted_out and stage >= 2). This state is reachable within 5 events.

Trace (initial: opted_out=F, sent=F, stage=0):
1. key_0 -> [t3] enabled (stage==0): stage:=1, sent:=F  => opted_out=F, sent=F, stage=1
2. key_0 -> [t6] enabled (stage==1): stage:=2, sent:=F  => opted_out=F, sent=F, stage=2
3. optout -> [t1] enabled (guard true): opted_out:=T, sent:=F  => opted_out=T, sent=F, stage=2
4. send_attempt -> on send_attempt the enabled transitions are checked: [t2] guard (not opted_out)=F, not enabled; [t9] guard (opted_out and stage<2)= T and F = F, not enabled; [t10] guard (opted_out and stage>=2)= T and T = T, enabled. [t10] fires: sent:=T  => opted_out=T, sent=T, stage=2.

Final reachable state: opted_out=true, sent=true. Here (opted_out implies not sent) is false, so the globally-property is violated. The counterexample uses 4 events (key_0, key_0, optout, send_attempt), within the 5-event bound. stage reaches 2 only via key_0 from stage 0 to 1 (t3) then key_0 from stage 1 to 2 (t6); optout (t1) fires unconditionally to set opted_out; then send_attempt triggers t10 because no other send_attempt transition is enabled. The design flaw is that t10 unconditionally re-enables sending for opted-out subjects once stage>=2, contradicting the intent that opted-out subjects must never have sent=true.
